title: API for incoming references
author: pyricau
body:
Mentioned in https://github.com/square/leakcanary/pull/2830#issuecomment-4835149315

We have to build careful, building incoming refs (aka backrefs) requires doing a graph traversal, and storing a list of refs for each instances, with more or less details (e.g. details could be inferred by rereading source objects on demand, but you might also want to know exactly how the graph traversal decided something was actually a valid ref, which involves details)

title: Support extracting thread dumps from heap dumps
author: pyricau
body:
HPROF heap dumps (from the JVM) can include a thread dump, but Shark currently has no API to extract it. This data isn't present in Android heap dumps, but there's no reason we couldn't support it for JVM dumps.

Came up in https://github.com/square/leakcanary/pull/2830#issuecomment-4815616967

title: Crash on devices running Android 17, due to Android 17 update
author: eneim
body:
### Description

Debug build running on Android 17 may crash, because:

- Android 17 prohibits modification of static final fields: https://developer.android.com/about/versions/17/behavior-changes-17#static-final-fields
- But Leakcanary does so: https://github.com/square/leakcanary/blob/0f9b924b4834ba63343253aca7240d6e521ac5cf/object-watcher/object-watcher-android-core/src/main/java/leakcanary/ServiceWatcher.kt#L144

### Steps to Reproduce

It crashes by simply running a debug build with leakcanary integrated, on a phone running Android 17. My case is on a Pixel 6 with Android 17 beta.

(I could have run the sample app but I think the issue is quite obvious here. Feel free to suggest a more clear reproduction steps).

**Expected behavior:** App launch without crahes on the Android 17 device.

### Version Information

* LeakCanary version: 2.14 (the code referenced above is from the main branch in this repo).
* Android OS version: 17
* Gradle version: 9.3.1

### Additional Information

Any additional information, configuration or data that might be necessary to reproduce the issue.

title: Kotlin multiplatform support
author: jQrgen
body:
### Problem description
I have a possible memory leak for Kotlin Multiplatform Android target running from commonTest: https://gitlab.com/wallywallet/wallet/-/jobs/14057416659

### Potential solutions
Support Kotlin Multiplatform for android only targets using `./gradlew pixel5Check`

### Additional information
Other targets such as iOS and JVM does not have to be initially supported.

title: Feature Proposal: Built MCP Server Integration for LeakCanary
author: ravisharma46
body:
Hi 👋 I’ve built an MCP server that exposes LeakCanary data for AI-assisted debugging and automation.
It allows structured access to leak traces, retained objects, and heap analysis.
This helps developers analyze memory leaks faster using tools like LLMs.
Repo: https://github.com/ravisharma46/Leak-Canary-Mcp-Server

title: Top content obscured by top bar in LeakCanary screens
author: tejas-bhong
body:
### Description

Parts of the UI content are overlapped by the top bar in multiple screens of the LeakCanary Android app, resulting in partially hidden text and reduced readability.
This is observed on:
1. Distinct Leaks screen
2. Leak trace details screen

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Install and open the LeakCanary app
2. Navigate to the Distinct Leaks screen
3. Open any leak trace
4. Observe the top portion of the content

**Expected behavior:** Content should be fully visible below the top bar and not overlap with it.

### Version Information

* LeakCanary version: 2.14
* Android OS version: 16
* Gradle version: 9.3.1

### Additional Information

- Device: Nothing Phone 3a

Screenshots attached for reference.
1. In this snapshot, top bar says 6 distinct leaks but, only 5 are visible as 1 is being obscured by the top bar.
![Screenshot_20260407-085424.png](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/57b22dd8-9c13-4366-a1d7-07e2b89edec4)
2. In this snapshot, top section with outline is cut and part of it's content is obscured by the top bar.
![Screenshot_20260407-085316.png](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/698015b1-f18c-4719-a5bf-d5ba24295326)

title: Floating action button partially hidden / overlapped by bottom navigation bar
author: Tkebug
body:
Bug Description:
The floating action button ("1 Distinct Leak") is being partially covered by the bottom navigation bar.

Observed Problems:
Only the top half of the button is visible. The bottom half is hidden behind the navigation bar ("Leaks", "Heap Dumps", "About").
The navigation bar buttons are overlapping the floating action button area, making part of the UI look clipped and unpolished.

Steps to Reproduce:
1) Open the Leak
2) Go to the main screen showing memory leaks.
3) Observe the floating action button.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fdfb6b6b-58e0-41d9-808b-f3d617da62a4)

Expected Result:
The floating action button should be fully visible with proper spacing/padding from the bottom navigation bar. The navigation bar should not overlap any interactive UI elements.

Actual Result:
The bottom portion of the floating action button is hidden behind the navigation bar. The button is still clickable, but the visual overlap creates a bad user experience.

Environment:
Device: Samsung Galaxy S24
OS: Android 16
App Version: LeakCanary 3.0-alpha-7

title: "Please grant request permission" is not very usable
author: Arthur-Milchior
body: ### Description I just installed the dev version of ankidroid, and rejected the request to grant permission to show notification. I got the following screen: The message can't be entirely read, which cause it to be mostly useless as far as explanation goes. I suggest using another form of in-app notification that don't limit to two lines. Ideally, I'd also consider making the notification tappable, so that if the user tap on it, they get to know exactly what is going on, and how to enable the notification now that they tapped on "reject". But since it's a tool for developers, I guess it's not absolutely needed, even if it would be nice to have. ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Install an app using leakcanary. 2. Use the app until the permission screen appear 3. reject the permission request **Expected behavior:** [What you expect to happen] I expect to see the full string. Or at least to be able to see it when I tap on the message. Ideally, to learn how to grant permission. Maybe I'd have liked to see an explanation of the permission request before it got asked, but that's not really necessary ### Version Information * LeakCanary version: 2.14 * Android OS version: 16 * Gradle version: `androidGradlePlugin = "8.13.2"` (not sure it's the value you need or whether I find another one) ### Additional Information Any additional information, configuration or data that might be necessary to reproduce the issue.

title: NegativeArraySizeException in HprofInMemoryIndex for large heap dumps (~7GB)
author: hurricup
body:
## Description

When opening a large heap dump (~6.9 GB) with `HprofHeapGraph.openHeapGraph()`, Shark crashes with a `NegativeArraySizeException` in `UnsortedByteEntries.append`. This appears to be an int overflow in the internal index builder when the heap dump contains a very large number of objects.

## Stack trace

```
java.lang.NegativeArraySizeException: -1545189232
	at shark.internal.UnsortedByteEntries.append(UnsortedByteEntries.kt:32)
	at shark.internal.HprofInMemoryIndex$Builder.onHprofRecord(HprofInMemoryIndex.kt:592)
	at shark.StreamingHprofReader.readRecords(StreamingHprofReader.kt:246)
	at shark.internal.HprofInMemoryIndex$Companion.indexHprof(HprofInMemoryIndex.kt:777)
	at shark.HprofIndex$Companion.indexRecordsOf(HprofIndex.kt:34)
	at shark.HprofHeapGraph$Companion.openHeapGraph(HprofHeapGraph.kt:428)
```

## Environment

- Shark version: 2.14 (`com.squareup.leakcanary:shark-graph:2.14`)
- JDK: JBR 21.0.8 (64-bit)
- Heap dump: ~6.9 GB, standard JVM hprof (JAVA PROFILE 1.0.2), from IntelliJ-based IDE
- OS: Linux x86_64

## Analysis

The negative array size `-1545189232` suggests an int overflow when computing a buffer/array size in `UnsortedByteEntries.append`. Smaller heap dumps (up to ~4.2 GB / 54M objects) work fine. This specific dump is ~6.9 GB and likely exceeds an int-sized capacity calculation.

## Reproducing

Any sufficiently large hprof file (likely >4GB or with enough records to overflow an int counter) should trigger this. The simplest reproduction:

```kotlin
val file = File("large-heap-dump.hprof")
file.openHeapGraph().use { graph ->
    // crashes before reaching this point
}
```

title: What should we do about unreachable objects in heap analysis result?
author: CoderGautam07
body:
I was trying to find memory leaks in my app using leak canary and as i was using the app i saw notification that more than 5 retained objects were found and head dump started but after it completed it said 0 retained objects but around 6 unreachable objects. Sometime even more or sometimes less. Now i am confused should i look into those unreachable objects or they are not any leaks and i should ignore them? I wanted an urgent resolution to my query thats why posted here Sorry!

0 APPLICATION LEAKS

References underlined with "~~~" are likely causes.
Learn more at https://squ.re/leaks.

0 LIBRARY LEAKS

See https://square.github.io/leakcanary/fundamentals-how-leakcanary-works/#4-categorizing-leaks

title: Duplicate namespace between leakcanary-android and leakcanary-android-process breaks AGP 9 builds
author: ygnessin
body:
### Description

With AGP 9.0.0, the build fails because both `leakcanary-android` and `leakcanary-android-process` declare the same namespace `com.squareup.leakcanary` in their manifests. 

AGP 9 enforces unique namespaces across dependencies, so `processDebugMainManifest` fails with:
> Namespace 'com.squareup.leakcanary' is used in multiple modules and/or libraries: com.squareup.leakcanary:leakcanary-android-process:2.14, com.squareup.leakcanary:leakcanary-android:2.14. Please ensure that all modules and libraries have a unique namespace. For more information, See https://developer.android.com/studio/build/configure-app-module#set-namespace

The only current workaround I'm aware of is to drop the leakcanary-android-process artifact losing multiprocess support.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Set up a project with `leakcanary-android` and `leakcanary-android-process`
2. Use Android Gradle Plugin version 9.0.0

### Version Information

* LeakCanary version: 2.14
* Android OS version: 16
* Gradle version: 9.2.1

### Additional Information

N/A
